richMetadata.structure: The Anunnaki are organized hierarchically under Anu (sky god). They inhabit multiple levels: high Anunnaki (council in heaven), middle Anunnaki (executive functions), and lower Anunnaki (workers). They gather in Nippur and convene in divine assembly (Ekur) for cosmic decisions.
connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth
richMetadata.significance: The Anunnaki represent divine bureaucracy and cosmic administration. They created humans to serve them and maintain cosmic order. Their existence illustrates Sumerian understanding of divine hierarchy, duty, and the interplay between celestial and terrestrial realms. The Anunnaki embody the principle of divine work and governance.
sources: Descent of Inanna - journey through realms
Cuneiform texts from Nippur and Sumeria

keyMyths.title: The Creation from Nun
keyMyths.description: In the beginning, there was only Nun, the primordial waters of chaos. From these waters, Atum, the self-created god, arose and initiated the creation of the world through acts of will and speech.

keyMyths.title: The Ogdoad
keyMyths.description: Before creation, the Ogdoad, a group of eight deities embodying the qualities of chaos (darkness, formlessness, boundlessness), existed within Nun. These deities eventually subsided, allowing for the ordered world to emerge.

keyMyths.title: The Sun God's Daily Journey
keyMyths.description: The sun god Ra travels across the sky each day in his solar barque, battling the serpent Apophis each night in the underworld (Duat). His daily rebirth symbolizes the constant cycle of creation and renewal.

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Nut and Geb
keyMyths.description: Nut, the sky goddess, and Geb, the earth god, were initially locked in a close embrace, preventing creation. Shu, the god of air, intervened and separated them, creating the space for the world to exist.

keyMyths.title: The Emergence of the Primeval Mound (Benben)
keyMyths.description: From the waters of Nun, a mound of earth, the Benben, arose. This was the first land and the place where creation began, often associated with the sun god and the act of bringing order from chaos.

sources.name: The Pyramid Texts
sources.description: The oldest known religious texts from ancient Egypt, found inscribed on the walls of pyramids.
sources.title: The Pyramid Texts

sources.name: The Coffin Texts
sources.description: A collection of funerary spells and religious texts inscribed on coffins during the Middle Kingdom.
sources.title: The Coffin Texts

sources.name: The Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells and rituals intended to guide the deceased through the afterlife.
sources.title: The Book of the Dead

sources.name: Frankfort, Henri. *Kingship and the Gods: A Study of Ancient Near Eastern Religion as the Integration of Society & Nature.*
sources.description: An influential study exploring the relationship between kingship and religion in the ancient Near East, including Egypt.
sources.title: Frankfort, Henri. *Kingship and the Gods: A Study of Ancient Near Eastern Religion as the Integration of Society & Nature.*

sources.name: Assmann, Jan. *Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt.*
sources.description: A comprehensive analysis of Egyptian funerary beliefs and the concept of the afterlife.
sources.title: Assmann, Jan. *Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt.*
